title: Untitled
research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) with new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency.
method_summary: Survey-based study using structured questionnaire administered in Dalian, China. Data analyzed with Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) using maximum likelihood estimation. Four models were tested: Model 1 (basic TPB: instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control), Model 2 (adding descriptive norm), Model 3 (adding perceived risk), and Model 4 (adding conformity tendency).
method_family: Quantitative, Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Survey research
datasets: 260 complete and valid questionnaire responses from pedestrians in Dalian, China
tasks: Predict pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intention (crossing against pedestrian traffic lights)
metrics: RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation)
baselines: Basic TPB model (Model 1) with instrumental attitude, subjective norm, and perceived behavioral control

main_claims.claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of violating crossing behavior intention in all four models (p<0.001 in all cases)
Model 4 (containing instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best model fit (CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030, CMIN/DF=1.235)
Subjective norm was significant in Model 1 (basic TPB) but became non-significant when descriptive norm was added to Model 2, indicating descriptive norm is a stronger predictor than subjective norm
Conformity tendency was a significant predictor of violating crossing behavior intention (p=0.004), indicating that pedestrians are influenced by other pedestrians' violating behavior
Perceived behavioral control and perceived risk were not significant predictors of violating crossing behavior intention in any model

limitations.text: Self-reported behavior may be biased, especially for negative behaviors
Relatively small number of items in each construct to maintain high internal reliability
Perceived behavioral control was not significant, contrary to TPB theory expectations - may need more risky scenarios to demonstrate effect

future_work: Increase the number of items in each construct to improve validity
Investigate perceived behavioral control effects under scenarios with different difficulty levels (wider roads, higher speed traffic)
Compare self-reported behavior with actual observed behavior for validation
notes: Study was conducted in Dalian, China with 260 respondents (57.3% female, 42.7% male); Model 4 with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency explained pedestrian violating crossing intentions better than basic TPB; Findings suggest pedestrians' observations of family/friends' behavior have greater influence than perceived approval from them; Results support using descriptive norms and conformity tendency in safety interventions to reduce pedestrian violations
